SUMMARY:
Small molecule OLEDs (SM-OLEDs) include tris(8-hydroxyquinolinato)aluminium fluorescent and phosphorescent dyes, and conjugated dendrimers.

## Contents

# Organic electronics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Organic_CMOS_logic_circuit.jpg/250px-Organic_CMOS_logic_circuit.jpg)

**Organic electronics** is a field of [materials science](/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science") concerning the design, [synthesis](/wiki/Chemical_synthesis "Chemical synthesis"), characterization, and application of [organic](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound") molecules or [polymers](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") that show desirable [electronic](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") properties such as [conductivity](/wiki/Electrical_resistivity_and_conductivity "Electrical resistivity and conductivity"). Unlike conventional inorganic [conductors](/wiki/Electrical_conductor "Electrical conductor") and [semiconductors](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor"), organic electronic materials are constructed from organic (carbon-based) molecules or polymers using synthetic strategies developed in the context of [organic chemistry](/wiki/Organic_chemistry "Organic chemistry") and [polymer chemistry](/wiki/Polymer_chemistry "Polymer chemistry").

One of the promised benefits of organic electronics is their potential low cost compared to traditional electronics.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Attractive properties of polymeric conductors include their electrical conductivity (which can be varied by the concentrations of [dopants](/wiki/Dopant "Dopant")) and comparatively high mechanical [flexibility](/wiki/Flexibility "Flexibility"). Challenges to the implementation of organic electronic materials are their inferior [thermal stability](/wiki/Thermal_stability "Thermal stability"), high cost, and diverse fabrication issues.

## History

### Electrically conductive polymers

Traditional conductive materials are [inorganic](/wiki/Inorganic "Inorganic"), especially [metals](/wiki/Metals "Metals") such as [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") and [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum "Aluminum") as well as many [alloys](/wiki/Alloy "Alloy").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1862 [Henry Letheby](/wiki/Henry_Letheby "Henry Letheby") described [polyaniline](/wiki/Polyaniline "Polyaniline"), which was subsequently shown to be electrically conductive. Work on other polymeric organic materials began in earnest in the 1960s. For example in 1963, a derivative of tetraiodopyrrole was shown to exhibit conductivity of 1 S/cm (S = [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens_(unit) "Siemens (unit)")).[[4]](#cite_note-4) In 1977, it was discovered that oxidation enhanced the conductivity of [polyacetylene](/wiki/Polyacetylene "Polyacetylene"). The 2000 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to [Alan J. Heeger](/wiki/Alan_J._Heeger "Alan J. Heeger"), [Alan G. MacDiarmid](/wiki/Alan_G._MacDiarmid "Alan G. MacDiarmid"), and [Hideki Shirakawa](/wiki/Hideki_Shirakawa "Hideki Shirakawa") jointly for their work on polyacetylene and related conductive polymers.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Many families of electrically conducting polymers have been identified including [polythiophene](/wiki/Polythiophene "Polythiophene"), [polyphenylene sulfide](/wiki/Polyphenylene_sulfide "Polyphenylene sulfide"), and others.

J.E. Lilienfeld[[6]](#cite_note-patent-6) first proposed the [field-effect transistor](/wiki/Field-effect_transistor "Field-effect transistor") in 1930, but the first OFET was not reported until 1987, when Koezuka et al. constructed one using [Polythiophene](/wiki/Polythiophene "Polythiophene")[[7]](#cite_note-Koezuka1988-7) which shows extremely high conductivity. Other [conductive polymers](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer") have been shown to act as semiconductors, and newly synthesized and characterized compounds are reported weekly in prominent research journals. Many review articles exist documenting the development of these [materials](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance").[[8]](#cite_note-sc-8)[[9]](#cite_note-pc-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-hk-12)

In 1987, the first organic [diode](/wiki/Diode "Diode") was produced at [Eastman Kodak](/wiki/Eastman_Kodak "Eastman Kodak") by [Ching W. Tang](/wiki/Ching_W._Tang "Ching W. Tang") and [Steven Van Slyke](/wiki/Steven_Van_Slyke "Steven Van Slyke").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Electrically conductive charge transfer salts

In the 1950s, organic molecules were shown to exhibit electrical conductivity. Specifically, the organic compound [pyrene](/wiki/Pyrene "Pyrene") was shown to form semiconducting charge-transfer complex [salts](/wiki/Salts "Salts") with [halogens](/wiki/Halogens "Halogens").[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 1972, researchers found metallic conductivity (conductivity comparable to a metal) in the charge-transfer complex [TTF-TCNQ](/wiki/TTF-TCNQ "TTF-TCNQ").

### Light and electrical conductivity

[André Bernanose](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Bernanose "André Bernanose")[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) was the first person to observe [electroluminescence](/wiki/Electroluminescence "Electroluminescence") in organic [materials](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance"). Ching W. Tang and [Steven Van Slyke](/wiki/Steven_Van_Slyke "Steven Van Slyke"),[[17]](#cite_note-ApplPhy87-17) reported fabrication of the first practical OLED device in 1987. The OLED device incorporated a double-layer structure motif composed of [copper phthalocyanine](/wiki/Copper_phthalocyanine "Copper phthalocyanine") and a derivative of [perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride](/wiki/Perylenetetracarboxylic_dianhydride "Perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 1990, a [polymer](/wiki/Poly(p-phenylene_vinylene) "Poly(p-phenylene vinylene)") light emitting diodes was demonstrated by [Bradley](/wiki/Donal_Bradley "Donal Bradley"), [Burroughes](/wiki/Jeremy_Burroughes "Jeremy Burroughes"), [Friend](/wiki/Richard_Friend "Richard Friend"). Moving from molecular to macromolecular materials solved the problems previously encountered with the long-term stability of the organic films and made high-quality films easy to produce.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In the late 1990's, highly efficient electroluminescent dopants were shown to dramatically increase the light-emitting efficiency of OLEDs[[20]](#cite_note-20) These results suggested that electroluminescent materials could displace traditional hot-filament lighting. Subsequent research developed multilayer polymers and the new field of plastic electronics and [organic light-emitting diodes](/wiki/Organic_light-emitting_diode "Organic light-emitting diode") (OLED) research and device production grew rapidly.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Conductive organic materials

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/SegStackEdgeOnHMTFCQ.jpg/250px-SegStackEdgeOnHMTFCQ.jpg)

Organic conductive materials can be grouped into two main classes: polymers and conductive [molecular](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") solids and salts. [Polycyclic aromatic](/wiki/Polycyclic_aromatic_hydrocarbon "Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon") compounds such as [pentacene](/wiki/Pentacene "Pentacene") and [rubrene](/wiki/Rubrene "Rubrene") often form semiconducting materials when partially oxidized.

[Conductive polymers](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer") are often typically intrinsically conductive or at least semiconductors. They sometimes show mechanical properties comparable to those of conventional organic polymers. Both [organic](/wiki/Organic_chemistry "Organic chemistry") synthesis and advanced [dispersion](/wiki/Dispersion_(chemistry) "Dispersion (chemistry)") techniques can be used to tune the [electrical](/wiki/Electrical "Electrical") properties of [conductive polymers](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer"), unlike typical [inorganic](/wiki/Inorganic "Inorganic") conductors. Well-studied class of [conductive polymers](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer") include [polyacetylene](/wiki/Polyacetylene "Polyacetylene"), [polypyrrole](/wiki/Polypyrrole "Polypyrrole"), [polythiophenes](/wiki/Polythiophene "Polythiophene"), and [polyaniline](/wiki/Polyaniline "Polyaniline"). Poly(p-phenylene vinylene) and its [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(chemistry) "Derivative (chemistry)") are [electroluminescent](/wiki/Electroluminescent "Electroluminescent") semiconducting polymers. Poly(3-alkythiophenes) have been incorporated into prototypes of [solar cells](/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell") and [transistors](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor").

## Organic light-emitting diode

An OLED (organic light-emitting diode) consists of a thin film of organic material that emits light under stimulation by an electric current. A typical OLED consists of an anode, a cathode, OLED organic material and a conductive layer.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Br6Acrystal.png/250px-Br6Acrystal.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Bilayer-OLED.png/250px-Bilayer-OLED.png)

OLED organic [materials](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance") can be divided into two major families: small-molecule-based and polymer-based. Small molecule OLEDs (SM-OLEDs) include [tris(8-hydroxyquinolinato)aluminium](/wiki/Tris(8-hydroxyquinolinato)aluminium "Tris(8-hydroxyquinolinato)aluminium")[[17]](#cite_note-ApplPhy87-17) [fluorescent](/wiki/Fluorescent "Fluorescent") and [phosphorescent](/wiki/Phosphorescent "Phosphorescent") dyes, and conjugated [dendrimers](/wiki/Dendrimers "Dendrimers"). [Fluorescent](/wiki/Fluorescent "Fluorescent") [dyes](/wiki/Dye "Dye") can be selected according to the desired range of [emission](/wiki/Emission_(radiocommunications) "Emission (radiocommunications)") [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength"); compounds like [perylene](/wiki/Perylene "Perylene") and [rubrene](/wiki/Rubrene "Rubrene") are often used. Devices based on small molecules are usually fabricated by [thermal](/wiki/Heat "Heat") [evaporation](/wiki/Evaporation "Evaporation") under [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum"). While this method enables the formation of well-controlled homogeneous [film](/wiki/Film "Film"); is hampered by high cost and limited scalability.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
[[25]](#cite_note-25) Polymer light-emitting diodes (PLEDs) are generally more efficient than SM-OLEDs. Common polymers used in PLEDs include [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(chemistry) "Derivative (chemistry)") of poly(p-phenylene vinylene)[[26]](#cite_note-Polyphenylene_vinylene-26) and [polyfluorene](/wiki/Polyfluorene "Polyfluorene"). The emitted [color](/wiki/Color "Color") is determined by the structure of the polymer. Compared to thermal evaporation, [solution](/wiki/Solution_(chemistry) "Solution (chemistry)")-based methods are more suited to creating [films](/wiki/Film "Film") with large dimensions.

## Organic field-effect transistor

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Rubrene.svg/250px-Rubrene.svg.png)

An organic field-effect transistor (OFET) is a field-effect transistor utilizing organic molecules or polymers as the active semiconducting layer. A field-effect transistor ([FET](/wiki/FET "FET")) is any semiconductor material that utilizes [electric field](/wiki/Electric_field "Electric field") to control the shape of a channel of one type of [charge](/wiki/Charge_(physics) "Charge (physics)") carrier, thereby changing its conductivity. Two major classes of [FET](/wiki/FET "FET") are n-type and p-type semiconductor, classified according to the charge type carried. In the case of organic FETs (OFETs), p-type OFET compounds are generally more stable than n-type due to the susceptibility of the latter to oxidative damage.

As for OLEDs, some OFETs are molecular and some are polymer-based system. [Rubrene](/wiki/Rubrene "Rubrene")-based OFETs show high carrier mobility of 20–40 cm2/(V·s). Another popular OFET material is [Pentacene](/wiki/Pentacene "Pentacene"). Due to its low [solubility](/wiki/Solubility "Solubility") in most organic [solvents](/wiki/Solvent "Solvent"), it's difficult to fabricate thin film transistors ([TFTs](/wiki/Thin-film_transistor "Thin-film transistor")) from pentacene itself using conventional spin-cast or, [dip coating](/wiki/Dip_coating "Dip coating") methods, but this obstacle can be overcome by using the derivative TIPS-pentacene.

## Organic electronic devices

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Flexible_display.jpg/250px-Flexible_display.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Organic_photovoltaic_material.pdf/page1-250px-Organic_photovoltaic_material.pdf.jpg)

[Organic solar cells](/wiki/Organic_solar_cell "Organic solar cell") could cut the cost of solar power compared with conventional solar-cell manufacturing.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Silicon [thin-film solar cells](/wiki/Thin-film_solar_cell "Thin-film solar cell") on flexible substrates allow a significant cost reduction of large-area photovoltaics for several reasons:[[28]](#cite_note-ipe.uni-stuttgart.de-28)

Inexpensive polymeric substrates like [polyethylene terephthalate](/wiki/Polyethylene_terephthalate "Polyethylene terephthalate") (PET) or [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate") (PC) have the potential for further cost reduction in photovoltaics. [Protomorphous](/wiki/Protocrystalline "Protocrystalline") solar cells prove to be a promising concept for efficient and low-cost photovoltaics on cheap and flexible substrates for large-area production as well as small and mobile applications.[[28]](#cite_note-ipe.uni-stuttgart.de-28)

One advantage of [printed electronics](/wiki/Printed_electronics "Printed electronics") is that different electrical and electronic components can be printed on top of each other, saving space and increasing reliability and sometimes they are all transparent. One ink must not damage another, and low temperature annealing is vital if low-cost flexible materials such as paper and [plastic film](/wiki/Plastic_film "Plastic film") are to be used. There is much sophisticated engineering and chemistry involved here, with iTi, Pixdro, Asahi Kasei, Merck & Co.|Merck, BASF, HC Starck, Sunew, Hitachi Chemical, and Frontier Carbon Corporation among the leaders.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
[Electronic devices](/wiki/Electronic_device "Electronic device") based on [organic compounds](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound") are now widely used, with many new products under development. [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") reported the first full-color, video-rate, flexible, plastic display made purely of organic [materials](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance");[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) [television](/wiki/Television "Television") screen based on OLED materials; [biodegradable](/wiki/Biodegradable "Biodegradable") electronics based on organic compound and low-cost organic [solar cell](/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell") are also available.

### Fabrication methods

Small molecule semiconductors are often [insoluble](/wiki/Insoluble "Insoluble"), necessitating [deposition](/wiki/Deposition_(chemistry) "Deposition (chemistry)") via vacuum [sublimation](/wiki/Sublimation_(phase_transition) "Sublimation (phase transition)"). Devices based on [conductive polymers](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer") can be prepared by solution processing methods. Both solution processing and vacuum based methods produce amorphous and [polycrystalline](/wiki/Polycrystalline "Polycrystalline") films with variable degree of disorder. "Wet" [coating](/wiki/Coating "Coating") techniques require polymers to be dissolved in a volatile [solvent](/wiki/Solvent "Solvent"), filtered and deposited onto a [substrate](/wiki/Substrate_(materials_science) "Substrate (materials science)"). Common examples of solvent-based coating techniques include drop casting, [spin-coating](/wiki/Spin-coating "Spin-coating"), doctor-blading, [inkjet printing](/wiki/Inkjet_printing "Inkjet printing") and [screen printing](/wiki/Screen_printing "Screen printing"). Spin-coating is a widely used technique for small area [thin film](/wiki/Thin_film "Thin film") production. It may result in a high degree of material loss. The doctor-blade technique results in a minimal material loss and was primarily developed for large area thin film production. Vacuum based thermal deposition of small molecules requires [evaporation](/wiki/Evaporation "Evaporation") of molecules from a hot source. The molecules are then transported through vacuum onto a substrate. The process of condensing these molecules on the substrate surface results in thin film formation. Wet coating techniques can in some cases be applied to small molecules depending on their solubility.

### Organic solar cells

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/BilayerElectrode.pdf/page1-250px-BilayerElectrode.pdf.jpg)

Organic semiconductor diodes convert light into electricity. Figure to the right shows five commonly used organic photovoltaic materials. Electrons in these organic molecules can be delocalized in a delocalized π [orbital](/wiki/Molecular_orbital "Molecular orbital") with a corresponding π\* antibonding [orbital](/wiki/Molecular_orbital "Molecular orbital"). The difference in energy between the π orbital, or highest occupied molecular orbital ([HOMO](/wiki/HOMO "HOMO")), and π\* orbital, or lowest unoccupied molecular orbital ([LUMO](/wiki/LUMO "LUMO")) is called the [band gap](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap") of organic photovoltaic materials. Typically, the [band gap](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap") lies in the range of 1-4eV.[[32]](#cite_note-Nelson-32)[[33]](#cite_note-HallsFriend-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Hoppe-34)

The difference in the [band gap](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap") of organic [photovoltaic](/wiki/Photovoltaic "Photovoltaic") materials leads to different chemical structures and forms of organic [solar cells](/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell"). Different forms of solar cells includes single-layer organic [photovoltaic](/wiki/Photovoltaic "Photovoltaic") cells, bilayer organic [photovoltaic](/wiki/Photovoltaic "Photovoltaic") cells and heterojunction [photovoltaic](/wiki/Photovoltaic "Photovoltaic") cells. However, all three of these types of solar cells share the approach of sandwiching the organic electronic layer between two metallic conductors, typically [indium tin oxide](/wiki/Indium_tin_oxide "Indium tin oxide").[[35]](#cite_note-McGehee-35)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Tft.png/250px-Tft.png)

### Organic field-effect transistors

An organic field-effect transistor is a three terminal device (source, drain and gate). The charge carriers move between source and drain, and the gate serves to control the path's conductivity. There are mainly two types of organic field-effect transistor, based on the semiconducting layer's charge transport, namely p-type (such as dinaphtho[2,3-*b*:2′,3′-*f*]thieno[3,2-*b*]thiophene, DNTT),[[36]](#cite_note-36) and n-type (such phenyl C61 butyric acid methyl ester, PCBM).[[37]](#cite_note-37) Certain organic semiconductors can also present both p-type and n-type (i.e., ambipolar) characteristics.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Such technology allows for the fabrication of large-area, flexible, low-cost electronics.[[39]](#cite_note-39) One of the main advantages is that being mainly a low temperature process compared to CMOS, different type of materials can be utilized. This makes them in turn great candidates for sensing.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Features

[Conductive polymers](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer") are lighter, more [flexible](/wiki/Flexible_electronics "Flexible electronics"), and less expensive than inorganic conductors. This makes them a desirable alternative in many applications. It also creates the possibility of new applications that would be impossible using copper or silicon.

Organic electronics not only includes [organic semiconductors](/wiki/Organic_semiconductor "Organic semiconductor"), but also organic [dielectrics](/wiki/Dielectric "Dielectric"), conductors and [light emitters](/wiki/Light_emitter "Light emitter").

New applications include [smart windows](/wiki/Smart_windows "Smart windows") and [electronic paper](/wiki/Electronic_paper "Electronic paper"). [Conductive polymers](/wiki/Conductive_polymer "Conductive polymer") are expected to play an important role in the emerging science of [molecular computers](/wiki/Molecular_computer "Molecular computer").

--- SOURCE 2: What are Organic Semiconductors? Examples, Applications and more ---
URL: https://www.ossila.com/pages/what-are-organic-semiconductors

SUMMARY:
Organic semiconductors are materials, ranging from small molecules to polymers, that can transport charge. As a result, delocalized electrons can "hop" between molecules in the organic semiconductor, enabling charge transport. These advantages make organic semiconductors stand out from traditional inorganic semiconductors like silicon, positioning them as attractive materials for various applications but especially lightweight and flexible electronic devices. Such features means organic semiconductors have very strong absorption and emission in the visible spectral range making them advantageous for organic electronic applications like photovoltaics and LEDs. Charge Transport in Organic Semiconductors High Exciton Binding Energy: The low dielectric constants of organic semiconductor materials means that electron-hole pairs are more tightly bound due to less shielding.

What are Organic Semiconductors?
================================

![Image 14: semiconductor](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/semiconductor_wafer.jpg?v=1728388349&width=1340&height=280&crop=center)
Organic semiconductors are materials, ranging from small molecules to polymers, that can transport charge. Unlike conducting materials, where electrons move freely across the material, organic semiconductors rely on a structure primarily composed of carbon and hydrogen atoms. These atoms are arranged to enable overlap of π-orbitals, allowing electron delocalization within each molecule. This delocalization facilitates electron movement, or conductivity, along the molecule. Additionally, the π-orbitals can overlap with those of neighboring molecules, creating intermolecular electronic coupling. As a result, delocalized electrons can "hop" between molecules in the organic semiconductor, enabling charge transport. These unique properties make organic semiconductors useful in applications like organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) and organic photovoltaics.

Main Classes of Organic Semiconductors
--------------------------------------

* * *

There are three main classes or types of organic semiconductor; small molecules, polymers and 2D materials. Within these broad categories there are families of organic semiconductor materials that are being explored within different areas of research from organic electronic devices to medical sensors. See the table below for the main classes of organic semiconductors and some example materials:

| Small Molecule Semiconductors | Polymeric Semiconductors | 2D Organic Semiconductors |
| --- | --- | --- |
| * Fullerenes * [Non-Fullerene Acceptors](https://www.ossila.com/collections/non-fullerene-acceptors "Non-fullerene acceptors, nfas") * Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) | * Classic conjugated polymers * Donor-Acceptor (D-A) copolymers * Polymeric blends | * 2D Covalent Organic Frameworks * Graphene Oxide / Reduced Graphene Oxide * Self-Assembled Monolayers |
![Image 15: examples of conjugation in organic semiconductors](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/conjugated-organic-semiconductors.svg?v=1731056960)

Examples of Conjugation in Organic Semiconductors

Organic small-molecule semiconductors as emmitters, active materials, and more./p>

Advantages of Organic Semiconductors
------------------------------------

* * *

Organic semiconductors offer several advantages, especially for applications in flexible and lightweight electronics. Here are some key benefits:

![Image 20: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Low-cost Preparation

![Image 21: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Light Weight

![Image 22: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Mechanical Flexibility

![Image 23: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Easy Processing

![Image 24: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Tunable Function Through Molecular Design

![Image 25: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Rich Availability

![Image 26: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Wide Range of Optical Properties

![Image 27: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Thin Film Formation

![Image 28: Check Mark](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/tick.svg?v=1613513177)Low Energy Consumption

These advantages make organic semiconductors stand out from traditional inorganic semiconductors like silicon, positioning them as attractive materials for various applications but especially lightweight and flexible electronic devices.

Chemistry of Organic Semiconductors
-----------------------------------

* * *

Organic semiconducting materials are versatile and can have many components. One of the most crucial components to organic semiconductors is conjugated units. This is where there is an area of sp 2-hybridised carbons. This kind of bonding means only three of the four valence electrons within carbon atoms hybridise. The fourth electron occupies the perpendicular p z orbital.

![Image 29: Delocalization of electrons in conjugated organic molecules](https://www.ossila.com/cdn/shop/files/delocalisation-in-conjugated-molecules.svg)

Delocalisation of electrons in conjugated organic molecules

Interactions between these p z orbitals form weaker π (bonding) and π* (anti-bonding) orbitals, which flatten the molecule. This planarity facilitates the deloc

enriched_points.expanded_content: Small molecules serve as key components in organic electronics, enabling charge transport, light emission, and photonic applications through their unique molecular structures and electronic properties.
Fullerenes, such as C60 and C70, are prominent small molecules used in organic photovoltaics and OLEDs due to their excellent electron-accepting capabilities and unique spherical structure.
Small molecules offer high purity, tunable electronic properties, and efficient charge transport, allowing for the creation of flexible, lightweight, and low-cost electronic devices.
Despite their advantages, small molecules can exhibit challenges such as high production costs, limited scalability in fabrication, and poorer thermal stability compared to polymeric counterparts.
